case_brief.variant_request: Generate isomorphic, easier, and harder follow-up practice variants.

changes: Changed constant term from 6 to 12 and linear coefficient from -5 to -7 while maintaining integer roots (3 and 4)
variants.intent: Practice factoring monic quadratics with integer roots using the same method as the original problem
variants.coach_note: This problem follows the exact same factoring pattern as the original. Find two numbers that multiply to the constant term and add to the linear coefficient.
answer_outline: Set each factor equal to zero

changes: Used smaller coefficients (linear term -3, constant 2) with simpler integer roots (1 and 2)
variants.intent: Practice factoring simpler monic quadratics with minimal calculation
variants.coach_note: This problem uses smaller numbers to reduce cognitive load while practicing the same factoring technique.
answer_outline: Set each factor equal to zero

changes: Introduced non-monic quadratic (leading coefficient 2) requiring additional factoring steps
variants.intent: Practice factoring quadratics with leading coefficients greater than 1
variants.coach_note: When the leading coefficient isn't 1, use the 'ac method' or trial-and-error to find factors. Verify by multiplying your factors.
answer_outline: Set each factor equal to zero

design_notes: Original problem was misclassified as linear in knowledge points but is actually quadratic; variants maintain quadratic focus
Isomorphic variant preserves problem structure while changing root values
Easier variant reduces coefficient magnitude while keeping monic form
Harder variant introduces non-monic quadratic to increase complexity
